Measles cases across the world

  • Show All Code
  • Hide All Code
  • View Source
Author

Carson Neubert, Ben Parker, Minh Ho-Hoang

Data Context

World Health Organization (WHO)

We are exploring measles and rubella cases across the world. The data was collected and published through the World Health Organization (WHO) portal, but for ease of access scripts to access the data were provided by tidytuesday.

WHO has published monthly measles and rubella data from 193 countries from June 2012 to 2025. The data is provisional and is early case reports by WHO member countries collected using each countries national surveillance systems. The data contains several types of case confirmations (suspected, clinical, epidemiologically linked, and lab confirmed) as well as population count data in the yearly data set. The data was collected to monitor measles and rubella cases worldwide in order to support public health decision making and vaccination strategies.

Code 
#needed libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(rvest)
library(here)
library(broom)
library(ggridges)
library(scales)

# reading in from github
cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')

# need to do some cleaning for the webscraping process
cases_month2 <- cases_month |>
  mutate(month_abb = recode_values(month, 
                        1 ~ "Jan",
                        2 ~ "Feb",
                        3 ~ "Mar",
                        4 ~ "Apr",
                        5 ~ "May",
                        6 ~ "Jun",
                        7 ~ "Jul",
                        8 ~ "Aug",
                        9 ~ "Sep",
                        10 ~ "Oct",
                        11 ~ "Nov",
                        12 ~ "Dec"),
      country_scrape = str_to_lower(country),
      country_scrape = str_replace_all(country_scrape, " ", "-"))

Weather and Climate Data

To investigate the relationship between seasonal patterns and measles cases, we’ve web scraped data provided by WeatherandClimate. WeatherAndClimate is an online climate zone and data finder for 80,000+ locations in the world based on Köppen climate classification. WeatherAndClimate does not disclose the exact source or data collection method. They provide historical records dating to 1980, but we will scrape mean monthly temperature and precipitation data across this time period. The data was collected to monitor Earth’s climate systems which have broad implications on socio-economic conditions.

Code 
country_vec <- c(unique(cases_month2$country_scrape))

grab_weather <- function(country) {
   base_url <- "https://weatherandclimate.com/"
   climate_url <- str_c(base_url,country) 
   
   result <- tryCatch({
       read_html(climate_url)
     }, error = function(e) {
       message("Failed for ", country, e$message)
       NULL
     })
 
   if (is.null(result)) {
     return(NULL) 
   } else {
     result |>
       html_element(".tb6") |>
       html_table() |>
       pivot_longer(col = Jan:Dec, names_to = "month", values_to = "stat") |> 
       filter(Month %in% c("Average precipitation mm (inches)", "Daily mean °C (°F)")) |>
       select(!Year) |>
       pivot_wider(names_from = "Month", values_from = "stat") |>
       rename(mean_temp = 2,
          mean_precip = 3) |>
       mutate(mean_temp = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_temp, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             mean_precip = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_precip, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             country = country) #got the regex case from AI looks for values in the ()
 
   }
 }
 
 scraped_weather <- map(.x = country_vec, .f = grab_weather) |>
   bind_rows(.id = "id")
 

# save for loading more than once
# write.csv(scraped_weather, "./site/data/weather-scrape.csv")

Country Demographic Dataset

The Country Demographic Dataset is taken from API Ninja’s Country API, which provides geographic, demographic, and economic statistics about every country in the world. API Ninjas publishes the data through its Country API, but their documentation does not specify the original primary collector of each statistic. Variables in the dataset include: population, GPD, surface area, unemployment, homicide rate, infant mortality, fertility, and more.

The observational unit in this dataset is a country, where each row represents one country and each column represents a country-level statistic. The exact dates at which country statistics were originally collected are not stated in the API documentation. Thus, the data used in this analysis represent the values returned by the API at the time we originally accessed it.

The data was collected for this project by sending requests to the API Ninjas Country API and retrieving the given JSON objects. Each of these JSON objects contains country level statistics, which were then put into a dataset where each country became one observation and each statistic became a variable/column.

The data was collected because it allows us to make comparisons between a country’s economic, demographic, or geographic characteristics and how prevalent measles is in those countries. We hypothesize that such factors are associated with changes in measles cases and have retrieved them from this API to asses our hypothesis.

Code 
country_names <- unique(cases_year$country)

api_key <- "hmz9wf1cpAko2WM4Fz3oPInaZIt44Uf9uh3VFfxD"

base_url <- "https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/country"

get_country_demo <- function(country_name){
  GET(url = base_url,
      add_headers('X-API-Key' = api_key),
      query = list(name = country_name)) |>
  content("text", encoding = "utf-8") |>
  fromJSON() |>
  as.data.frame()
}

country_demographic <- map_dfr(country_names, get_country_demo)
# after the join... 2756 of the 22,780 datapoints don't have temp or precip data
# 19 of the 173 countries failed in the iteration
# cleaning script to not need api calls
# country_demographic_clean <- country_demographic |>
#   unnest(currency, names_sep = "_")
# 
# write_csv(country_demographic_clean, "data/country_demographic.csv")

Final Dataset

Code 
api_load <- read.csv("./data/country_demographic.csv")
# load csv climate data
scraped_data <- read.csv(here("data", "weather-scrape.csv")) 

cases_month3 <- left_join(cases_month2, scraped_data, by = c("month_abb" = "month",  "country_scrape" = "country" ), 
                          relationship = "many-to-many")
meta_data <- left_join(cases_month3,
                       api_load,
                       by = c("country" = "name"),
                       relationship = "many-to-many")

Research Questions

Climate, Geography and Measles Rates

Our first analysis aims to isolate trends in measles rates across climatic factors, natural resources and geography. Specificially we aim to answer:

  • Are there seasonal patterns in measles outbreaks across different regions?

  • How are climate, natural resources and emissions related to measles/rubella cases?

  • How are total measles cases spatially distributed throughout the world’s regions?

To better understand the relationship between climate and measles/rubella cases, we will explore monthly mean lab confirmed measles/rubella cases in the northern and southern hemisphere. We will ignore changes across years, and focus on monthly variation. We will compare India and Indonesia, who are both in the top 10 countries with the worst measles outbreaks as of 2023.

Code 
# Use of unmodified column, custom function
monthly_sums <- function(df, group, countries) {
  if (is.data.frame(df)) {
    df |>
      filter(country %in% c(countries)) |>
      group_by({{group}}, country) |>
      summarize(
                `Mean Measles Cases` = mean(measles_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                `Mean Rubella Cases` = mean(rubella_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                .groups = "drop") |>
      mutate(month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
      arrange(month_abb) |>
      pivot_longer(cols = c(`Mean Measles Cases`, `Mean Rubella Cases`),
                   names_to =  "Statistic",
                   values_to = "value") |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = month_abb,
                  values_from = value) |>
      group_by(country) |>
      gt() |>
      tab_style(
        style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
        locations = list(cells_column_labels(), cells_row_groups())) |> 
      tab_header(
        title = md("Mean Lab Confirmed Measles and Rubella Cases Per Month")) |>
      tab_source_note(
        source_note = md("*Table 1: Mean monthly lab-confirmed measles and rubella cases over the course of the study (2012-2025).*")) |>
      fmt_number(
        columns  = where(is.numeric),
        decimals = 1) |>
      data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
  } else {
  stop('Must input a dataframe')
  }
}

monthly_sums(df = meta_data, 
             group = month_abb,
            countries = c("Indonesia" , "India"))
Mean Lab Confirmed Measles and Rubella Cases Per Month
Statistic Jan Feb Mar Apr May Jun Jul Aug Sep Oct Nov Dec
India
Mean Measles Cases 790.8 824.2 993.8 792.0 554.4 325.1 268.5 255.0 290.7 362.3 576.4 750.5
Mean Rubella Cases 102.2 139.9 145.5 131.5 88.4 87.1 98.3 89.5 114.0 111.5 131.8 129.8
Indonesia
Mean Measles Cases 201.2 189.1 205.8 149.4 125.8 87.1 123.2 132.3 143.7 116.3 111.1 63.7
Mean Rubella Cases 76.5 72.8 71.1 59.2 48.6 28.8 20.8 19.7 26.8 36.9 28.5 18.2
Table 1: Mean monthly lab-confirmed measles and rubella cases over the course of the study (2012-2025).

From our analysis we see a strong relationship between month and measles/rubella cases. This confirms the long held notion of a northern hemisphere “flu season” during the winter. The relationship in Indonesia is more subtle and may be indicative of their tropical climate.

Next, we will next investigate how climate, natural resources and emissions are related to measles/rubella cases.

Code 
library(corrr)
make_titles <- function(x){
  # expects a character or factor as an input
  stopifnot(is.character(x) | is.factor(x))
  
  # remove the units at the end of the name
  str_remove(x, pattern = "(g|mm)$") |> 
  # replace all _ with spaces
  str_replace_all(pattern = "_", replacement = " ") |> 
  # remove extra whitespace on left 
  str_trim(side = "both") |> 
  # convert the first letter of each word to upper case
  str_to_title()
}

meta_data |>
  select(month, measles_total, measles_epi_linked, rubella_total, 
         rubella_epi_linked, mean_temp, mean_precip, surface_area,
         forested_area, co2_emissions, threatened_species, internet_users) |>
  correlate(method = "pearson",
            use = "pairwise.complete.obs") |>
  rename(term1 = term) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = -term1, 
               names_to = "term2", 
               values_to = "correlation") |>
  mutate(
    across(.cols = c(term1, term2), 
           .fns = ~ make_titles(.x)
           )
    ) |> 
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = term1,
                       y = term2, 
                       fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(x = "", 
       y = "", 
       title = "Correlation Between Measles/Rubella Cases and Biological Factors", 
       fill = "Correlation"
       ) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B")

When comparing climate and natural resources, we see expected results such as a positive correlation between mean precipitation and forested area. However, we found suprising results indicating a positive correlation between Co2 emissions and measles/rubella cases (Figure 1).

Finally, we investigate spatial differences in measles cases from last year and compare the distributions of total measles cases across world regions (Figure 2).

Code 
regions_df <- cases_year |>
  mutate(region = recode_values(region,
                    "EMRO" ~ "Eastern Mediterranean",
                    "AFRO" ~ "Africa",
                    "EURO" ~ "Europe",
                    "SEARO" ~ "South-East Asia",
                    "AMRO" ~ "Americas",
                    "WPRO" ~ "Western Pacific"))

region_burden <- function(region_name) {
  regions_df |>
    filter(year == 2025, region == region_name) |>
    mutate(measles_total = as.numeric(measles_total)) |>
    summarize(
      region = region_name,
      total_cases = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
}

regions <- unique(regions_df$region)

map(regions, region_burden) |>
  bind_rows(.id="id") |> 
  mutate(pct_global = round(total_cases / sum(total_cases) * 100, 1)) |>
  arrange(desc(total_cases)) |>
  select(!id) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Regional Measles Burden**"),
    subtitle = "2025 Contribution to Global Total"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    region      = "Region",
    total_cases = "Total Cases",
    pct_global  = "% of Global Total"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_cases, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = pct_global,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern  = "{x}%"
    ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("*Table 2: Total measles cases and share of global burden by WHO region, 2025.*")
  ) |>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
Regional Measles Burden
2025 Contribution to Global Total
Region Total Cases % of Global Total
Eastern Mediterranean 31,052 34.9%
Africa 18,832 21.2%
Europe 18,551 20.9%
South-East Asia 10,856 12.2%
Americas 5,340 6.0%
Western Pacific 4,222 4.8%
Table 2: Total measles cases and share of global burden by WHO region, 2025.
Code 
regions_df |>
  filter(measles_total <= 5000) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_total, y = fct_reorder(region, measles_total, 
                                                .desc = FALSE), )) +
  geom_density_ridges(aes(fill = region),
                      alpha = 0.5, scale = 1,
                      show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Total Measles Cases per Year", 
       y = "", 
       title = "Comparison of Measles Cases across World Regions"
       ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "B")

Measles Cases From Country to Country

Does the impact of measles vary across countries? If so, what factors might contribute to those differences?

We first examined the relationship between a country’s economic standing and its measles cases. Since graphing all 200 countries would produce a cluttered visualization, we focused on the five richest and five poorest countries and compared how their measles cases changed over time. These countries were identified using a Wikipedia page ranking countries by GDP (PPP) per capita.

Code 
richest_5 <- c("Singapore", "Ireland", "Luxembourg", "Norway", "Qatar")
poorest_5 <- c("Burundi", "Central African Republic", "South Sudan", "Yemen", "Mozambique")

timeseries_df <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, 1),
  measles_per_100k = (measles_total / (population * 1000)) * 100000  ) |>
  filter(country %in% c(richest_5, poorest_5)) |>
  mutate(
    wealth_group = case_when(
      country %in% richest_5 ~ "Richest 5",
      country %in% poorest_5 ~ "Poorest 5"
    )
  )
Code 
ggplot(timeseries_df, aes(x = date, y = measles_per_100k, group = country, color = wealth_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Richest 5" = "steelblue4", "Poorest 5" = "firebrick3")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Adjusted Measles Rates Over Time (2012-2025)",
    subtitle = "Top 5 richest vs bottom 5 poorest countries",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Measles Cases per 100,000 People",
    color = "Group") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom")

From 2012 to 2025, the poorest five countries experienced higher measles case counts than the richest five countries. While measles cases in the richest group remained close to zero in the past 13 years, the poorest group showed repeated outbreak spikes, with large increases in more recent times. This suggests a strong association GDP and measles cases.

While the time series plot compares measles rates between the richest and poorest country groups, it does not show the size of the economic difference between those groups. The following table will take a deeper dive into this discrepancy.

Code 
gdppc_for_countries <- function(data, rich_countries, poor_countries) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      gdp_per_capita = (gdp / population) * 1000,
      group = case_when(
        country %in% rich_countries ~ "Richest 5",
        country %in% poor_countries ~ "Poorest 5")) |>
    filter(country %in% c(rich_countries, poor_countries)) |>
    group_by(group, country) |>
    arrange(desc(year), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    summarize(
      gdp_per_capita_usd = first(gdp_per_capita),
      .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(group, desc(gdp_per_capita_usd)) |>
    gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
    fmt_currency(
      columns = gdp_per_capita_usd,
      currency = "USD",
      decimals = 0) |>
    cols_label(
      country = "Country",
      gdp_per_capita_usd = "GDP per Capita (USD)") |>
    tab_header(
      title = md("**GDP per Capita of Selected Richest and Poorest Countries**"),
      subtitle = "Countries grouped using GDP per capita rankings") |>
    tab_source_note(
      source_note = "GDP per capita was calculated as GDP divided by population.
      GDP appears to be stored in millions of USD, and population appears to be stored in thousands.") |>
    tab_options(table.width = pct(65))}

gdppc_for_countries(meta_data, richest_5, poorest_5)
GDP per Capita of Selected Richest and Poorest Countries
Countries grouped using GDP per capita rankings
Country GDP per Capita (USD)
Poorest 5
Yemen $894
South Sudan $733
Mozambique $471
Central African Republic $464
Burundi $276
Richest 5
Luxembourg $113,291
Norway $80,090
Ireland $77,496
Qatar $66,422
Singapore $61,729
GDP per capita was calculated as GDP divided by population. GDP appears to be stored in millions of USD, and population appears to be stored in thousands.

It is important to note that because the countries were grouped using GDP per capita rankings, an economic gap between the two groups is expected. However, the size of this gap is not immediately visible from the time series plot alone. Based on the table, the selected richest countries have approximately 150 times higher GDP per capita than the selected poorest countries, highlighting the large economic difference between the groups.

Data Model

Code 
linearreg_df <- meta_data |>
  select(
    country,
    year,
    gdp,
    population,
    measles_total,
    mean_temp,
    urban_population,
    tourists) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_per_capita = gdp / population,
    measles_rate_100k = (measles_total / population) * 100000,
    log_gdp_per_capita = log(gdp_per_capita),
    log_measles_rate_100k = log(measles_rate_100k + 1),
    log_tourists = log(tourists))
Code 
  lm(
    log_measles_rate_100k ~ log_gdp_per_capita + mean_temp + log_tourists + urban_population, 
    data = linearreg_df) |>
  tidy() |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    term = md("**Predictor**"),
    estimate = md("**Estimate**"),
    std.error = md("**Std. Error**"),
    statistic = md("**t-value**"),
    p.value = md("**p-value**")
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Regression Model Results**"),
    subtitle = "Predicting logged measles case rate per 100,000"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Linear regression using GDP per capita, mean temperature, number of tourists, and urban population as predictors.")
Regression Model Results
Predicting logged measles case rate per 100,000
Predictor Estimate Std. Error t-value p-value
(Intercept) 1.327 0.143 9.275 0.000
log_gdp_per_capita −0.408 0.024 −17.129 0.000
mean_temp 0.010 0.001 8.213 0.000
log_tourists 0.196 0.013 15.187 0.000
urban_population −0.003 0.001 −1.983 0.047
Linear regression using GDP per capita, mean temperature, number of tourists, and urban population as predictors.

Original

To be answered by the data:

  • How does the volume of measles cases change over time?

  • Which areas of the world contribute the most to measles cases?

To be answered using supplemental data:

  • How does vaccination rates affect measles cases by country? (using vaccination data sets likely from WHO)

  • Are measles case rates correlated with GPD and development level of a country? (get GDP and country data from somewhere like gapminder)

Summary 2
- Using measels_year, which regions of the world contribute the most to measles cases? (only looking at 2025)

  • Use of custom function, iteration, 1 unmodified column, 1 modified column

Summary 3: Average lab quality across each year.

Average lab quality is the proportion of lab confirmed measles cases and total reported measles cases (across all testing types).

Code 
years <- sort(unique(cases_year$year))

get_lab_quality <- function(y){
  cases_year |>
    filter(year == y)|>
    mutate(lab_quality = measles_lab_confirmed / measles_total) |>
    summarise(avg_lab_quality = mean(lab_quality, na.rm = TRUE),
              .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(year = y)
}

map(years, get_lab_quality) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  select(year, avg_lab_quality) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = md("**Lab Confirmation Quality Over Time**")) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = avg_lab_quality, decimals = 1) |>
  cols_label(
    year = "Year",
    avg_lab_quality = "Average Lab Confirmation Share") |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("*Table 3: Average lab confirmation share by year (2012–2025)*"))
Lab Confirmation Quality Over Time
Year Average Lab Confirmation Share
2012 63.6%
2013 62.0%
2014 63.6%
2015 64.2%
2016 64.9%
2017 66.5%
2018 64.4%
2019 69.5%
2020 66.5%
2021 60.5%
2022 66.8%
2023 74.6%
2024 75.4%
2025 73.8%
Table 3: Average lab confirmation share by year (2012–2025)

Is there a relationship between mean temperature measles cases throughout the year?

Code 
meta_data |>
  group_by(month, month_abb) |>
  summarize(
    mean_measles = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_temp = mean(mean_temp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    mean_measles = as.numeric(scale(mean_measles)),
    mean_temp = as.numeric(scale(mean_temp)),
    month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_measles, mean_temp),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month_abb, y = value, color = variable, group = variable)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Measles Cases and Temperature by Month",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Standardized Value",
    color = "Variable") +
  theme_minimal()

Is there a relationship between a country’s GDP and Measles cases?

Code 
meta_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    mean_measles = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    gdp_per_capita = mean(gdp_per_capita, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(mean_measles != 0 & gdp_per_capita != 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_measles)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "Country GDP per Capita and Mean Measles Cases",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean Measles Cases (log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()
Code 
library(scales)

meta_data |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(annual_measles = sum(measles_total, na.rm=TRUE),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region.x),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(mean_annual_measles = mean(annual_measles),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region),
            .grpups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_annual_measles)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 20) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma_format()) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "blueviolet",
                      high = "goldenrod") +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country",
    x = "GDP per capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean annual measles cases (log scale)",
    fill = "No. Countries",
    alt = paste(
      "Hexbin plot of countries comparing GDP per capita and mean annual measles
      cases.",
      "Both axes are on log scale.",
      "Contries are concentrated along a negatively correlated line, with many 
      contries having low GDP and high measles cases, while many others have low
      measles cases and high GDP."
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw()
Source Code
---
title: "Measles cases across the world"
author: "Carson Neubert, Ben Parker, Minh Ho-Hoang"
format: 
  html:
    embed-resources: true
    code-tools: true
    page-layout: full
    toc: true 
    code-fold: true
    editor: visual
execute:
  message: false
  warning: false
---

![](./measles.jpg)

## Data Context

#### World Health Organization (WHO)

We are exploring measles and rubella cases across the world. The data was collected and [published](https://immunizationdata.who.int/global?topic=Provisional-measles-and-rubella-data&location=) through the World Health Organization (WHO) portal, but for ease of access scripts to access the data were provided by [tidytuesday](https://github.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/blob/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/readme.md).

WHO has published monthly measles and rubella data from 193 countries from June 2012 to 2025. The data is provisional and is early case reports by WHO member countries collected using each countries national surveillance systems. The data contains several types of case confirmations (suspected, clinical, epidemiologically linked, and lab confirmed) as well as population count data in the yearly data set. The data was collected to monitor measles and rubella cases worldwide in order to support public health decision making and vaccination strategies.

```{r}
#needed libraries
library(tidyverse)
library(gt)
library(httr)
library(tidyjson)
library(jsonlite)
library(rvest)
library(here)
library(broom)
library(ggridges)
library(scales)

# reading in from github
cases_month <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_month.csv')
cases_year <- readr::read_csv('https://raw.githubusercontent.com/rfordatascience/tidytuesday/main/data/2025/2025-06-24/cases_year.csv')

# need to do some cleaning for the webscraping process
cases_month2 <- cases_month |>
  mutate(month_abb = recode_values(month, 
                        1 ~ "Jan",
                        2 ~ "Feb",
                        3 ~ "Mar",
                        4 ~ "Apr",
                        5 ~ "May",
                        6 ~ "Jun",
                        7 ~ "Jul",
                        8 ~ "Aug",
                        9 ~ "Sep",
                        10 ~ "Oct",
                        11 ~ "Nov",
                        12 ~ "Dec"),
      country_scrape = str_to_lower(country),
      country_scrape = str_replace_all(country_scrape, " ", "-"))
```

#### Weather and Climate Data

To investigate the relationship between seasonal patterns and measles cases, we've web scraped data provided by [WeatherandClimate](https://weatherandclimate.com/). WeatherAndClimate is an online climate zone and data finder for 80,000+ locations in the world based on Köppen climate classification. WeatherAndClimate does not disclose the exact source or data collection method. They provide historical records dating to 1980, but we will scrape mean monthly temperature and precipitation data across this time period. The data was collected to monitor Earth's climate systems which have broad implications on socio-economic conditions.

```{r}
#| eval: false

country_vec <- c(unique(cases_month2$country_scrape))

grab_weather <- function(country) {
   base_url <- "https://weatherandclimate.com/"
   climate_url <- str_c(base_url,country) 
   
   result <- tryCatch({
       read_html(climate_url)
     }, error = function(e) {
       message("Failed for ", country, e$message)
       NULL
     })
 
   if (is.null(result)) {
     return(NULL) 
   } else {
     result |>
       html_element(".tb6") |>
       html_table() |>
       pivot_longer(col = Jan:Dec, names_to = "month", values_to = "stat") |> 
       filter(Month %in% c("Average precipitation mm (inches)", "Daily mean °C (°F)")) |>
       select(!Year) |>
       pivot_wider(names_from = "Month", values_from = "stat") |>
       rename(mean_temp = 2,
          mean_precip = 3) |>
       mutate(mean_temp = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_temp, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             mean_precip = as.numeric(str_extract(mean_precip, "(?<=\\()[0-9.]+")),
             country = country) #got the regex case from AI looks for values in the ()
 
   }
 }
 
 scraped_weather <- map(.x = country_vec, .f = grab_weather) |>
   bind_rows(.id = "id")
 

# save for loading more than once
# write.csv(scraped_weather, "./site/data/weather-scrape.csv")

```

#### Country Demographic Dataset

The Country Demographic Dataset is taken from API Ninja's Country API, which provides geographic, demographic, and economic statistics about every country in the world. API Ninjas publishes the data through its Country API, but their documentation does not specify the original primary collector of each statistic. Variables in the dataset include: population, GPD, surface area, unemployment, homicide rate, infant mortality, fertility, and more.

The observational unit in this dataset is a country, where each row represents one country and each column represents a country-level statistic. The exact dates at which country statistics were originally collected are not stated in the API documentation. Thus, the data used in this analysis represent the values returned by the API at the time we originally accessed it.

The data was collected for this project by sending requests to the API Ninjas Country API and retrieving the given JSON objects. Each of these JSON objects contains country level statistics, which were then put into a dataset where each country became one observation and each statistic became a variable/column.

The data was collected because it allows us to make comparisons between a country's economic, demographic, or geographic characteristics and how prevalent measles is in those countries. We hypothesize that such factors are associated with changes in measles cases and have retrieved them from this API to asses our hypothesis.

```{r}
#| eval: false

country_names <- unique(cases_year$country)

api_key <- "hmz9wf1cpAko2WM4Fz3oPInaZIt44Uf9uh3VFfxD"

base_url <- "https://api.api-ninjas.com/v1/country"

get_country_demo <- function(country_name){
  GET(url = base_url,
      add_headers('X-API-Key' = api_key),
      query = list(name = country_name)) |>
  content("text", encoding = "utf-8") |>
  fromJSON() |>
  as.data.frame()
}

country_demographic <- map_dfr(country_names, get_country_demo)
# after the join... 2756 of the 22,780 datapoints don't have temp or precip data
# 19 of the 173 countries failed in the iteration
# cleaning script to not need api calls
# country_demographic_clean <- country_demographic |>
#   unnest(currency, names_sep = "_")
# 
# write_csv(country_demographic_clean, "data/country_demographic.csv")
```

#### Final Dataset

```{r}
api_load <- read.csv("./data/country_demographic.csv")
# load csv climate data
scraped_data <- read.csv(here("data", "weather-scrape.csv")) 

cases_month3 <- left_join(cases_month2, scraped_data, by = c("month_abb" = "month",  "country_scrape" = "country" ), 
                          relationship = "many-to-many")
meta_data <- left_join(cases_month3,
                       api_load,
                       by = c("country" = "name"),
                       relationship = "many-to-many")
```

## Research Questions

### Climate, Geography and Measles Rates

Our first analysis aims to isolate trends in measles rates across climatic factors, natural resources and geography. Specificially we aim to answer:

-   Are there seasonal patterns in measles outbreaks across different regions?

-   How are climate, natural resources and emissions related to measles/rubella cases?

-   How are total measles cases spatially distributed throughout the world's regions?

To better understand the relationship between climate and measles/rubella cases, we will explore monthly mean lab confirmed measles/rubella cases in the northern and southern hemisphere. We will ignore changes across years, and focus on monthly variation. We will compare India and Indonesia, who are both in the [top 10 countries](https://www.cdc.gov/global-measles-vaccination/data-research/global-measles-outbreaks/index.html) with the worst measles outbreaks as of 2023.

```{r}
# Use of unmodified column, custom function
monthly_sums <- function(df, group, countries) {
  if (is.data.frame(df)) {
    df |>
      filter(country %in% c(countries)) |>
      group_by({{group}}, country) |>
      summarize(
                `Mean Measles Cases` = mean(measles_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                `Mean Rubella Cases` = mean(rubella_lab_confirmed, na.rm = TRUE),
                .groups = "drop") |>
      mutate(month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
      arrange(month_abb) |>
      pivot_longer(cols = c(`Mean Measles Cases`, `Mean Rubella Cases`),
                   names_to =  "Statistic",
                   values_to = "value") |>
      pivot_wider(names_from = month_abb,
                  values_from = value) |>
      group_by(country) |>
      gt() |>
      tab_style(
        style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
        locations = list(cells_column_labels(), cells_row_groups())) |> 
      tab_header(
        title = md("Mean Lab Confirmed Measles and Rubella Cases Per Month")) |>
      tab_source_note(
        source_note = md("*Table 1: Mean monthly lab-confirmed measles and rubella cases over the course of the study (2012-2025).*")) |>
      fmt_number(
        columns  = where(is.numeric),
        decimals = 1) |>
      data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )
  } else {
  stop('Must input a dataframe')
  }
}

monthly_sums(df = meta_data, 
             group = month_abb,
            countries = c("Indonesia" , "India"))
```

From our analysis we see a strong relationship between month and measles/rubella cases. This confirms the long held notion of a northern hemisphere "flu season" during the winter. The relationship in Indonesia is more subtle and may be indicative of their tropical climate.

Next, we will next investigate how climate, natural resources and emissions are related to measles/rubella cases.

```{r}
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-alt: "A plot showing the relationship between climate, natural resources and emissions data and measles/rubella cases."
#| fig-cap: "Figure 1: Correlation matrix showing the relationship between climate, natural resources and emissions data and measles/rubella cases."


library(corrr)
make_titles <- function(x){
  # expects a character or factor as an input
  stopifnot(is.character(x) | is.factor(x))
  
  # remove the units at the end of the name
  str_remove(x, pattern = "(g|mm)$") |> 
  # replace all _ with spaces
  str_replace_all(pattern = "_", replacement = " ") |> 
  # remove extra whitespace on left 
  str_trim(side = "both") |> 
  # convert the first letter of each word to upper case
  str_to_title()
}

meta_data |>
  select(month, measles_total, measles_epi_linked, rubella_total, 
         rubella_epi_linked, mean_temp, mean_precip, surface_area,
         forested_area, co2_emissions, threatened_species, internet_users) |>
  correlate(method = "pearson",
            use = "pairwise.complete.obs") |>
  rename(term1 = term) |> 
  pivot_longer(cols = -term1, 
               names_to = "term2", 
               values_to = "correlation") |>
  mutate(
    across(.cols = c(term1, term2), 
           .fns = ~ make_titles(.x)
           )
    ) |> 
  ggplot(mapping = aes(x = term1,
                       y = term2, 
                       fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile() +
  labs(x = "", 
       y = "", 
       title = "Correlation Between Measles/Rubella Cases and Biological Factors", 
       fill = "Correlation"
       ) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(option = "B")

```

When comparing climate and natural resources, we see expected results such as a positive correlation between mean precipitation and forested area. However, we found suprising results indicating a positive correlation between Co2 emissions and measles/rubella cases (Figure 1).

Finally, we investigate spatial differences in measles cases from last year and compare the distributions of total measles cases across world regions (Figure 2).

```{r}
regions_df <- cases_year |>
  mutate(region = recode_values(region,
                    "EMRO" ~ "Eastern Mediterranean",
                    "AFRO" ~ "Africa",
                    "EURO" ~ "Europe",
                    "SEARO" ~ "South-East Asia",
                    "AMRO" ~ "Americas",
                    "WPRO" ~ "Western Pacific"))

region_burden <- function(region_name) {
  regions_df |>
    filter(year == 2025, region == region_name) |>
    mutate(measles_total = as.numeric(measles_total)) |>
    summarize(
      region = region_name,
      total_cases = sum(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE)
    )
}

regions <- unique(regions_df$region)

map(regions, region_burden) |>
  bind_rows(.id="id") |> 
  mutate(pct_global = round(total_cases / sum(total_cases) * 100, 1)) |>
  arrange(desc(total_cases)) |>
  select(!id) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(
    title    = md("**Regional Measles Burden**"),
    subtitle = "2025 Contribution to Global Total"
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    region      = "Region",
    total_cases = "Total Cases",
    pct_global  = "% of Global Total"
  ) |>
  fmt_number(columns = total_cases, decimals = 0) |>
  fmt_number(
    columns  = pct_global,
    decimals = 1,
    pattern  = "{x}%"
    ) |>
  tab_style(
    style     = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
    locations = cells_column_labels()
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("*Table 2: Total measles cases and share of global burden by WHO region, 2025.*")
  ) |>
  data_color(
        columns = everything(),
        palette = "Oranges"
       )


```

```{r}
#| fig-height: 8
#| fig-width: 15
#| fig-alt: "A plot showing the distributions of measles cases per year across world regions."
#| fig-cap: "Figure 2: Comparison of the distribution of measles cases per year across world regions."

regions_df |>
  filter(measles_total <= 5000) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = measles_total, y = fct_reorder(region, measles_total, 
                                                .desc = FALSE), )) +
  geom_density_ridges(aes(fill = region),
                      alpha = 0.5, scale = 1,
                      show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Total Measles Cases per Year", 
       y = "", 
       title = "Comparison of Measles Cases across World Regions"
       ) +
  theme(legend.position = "none") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = label_comma()) +
  theme_bw() +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(option = "B")

```

### Measles Cases From Country to Country

Does the impact of measles vary across countries? If so, what factors might contribute to those differences?

We first examined the relationship between a country’s economic standing and its measles cases. Since graphing all 200 countries would produce a cluttered visualization, we focused on the five richest and five poorest countries and compared how their measles cases changed over time. These countries were identified using a [Wikipedia page](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita) ranking countries by GDP (PPP) per capita.

```{r}
richest_5 <- c("Singapore", "Ireland", "Luxembourg", "Norway", "Qatar")
poorest_5 <- c("Burundi", "Central African Republic", "South Sudan", "Yemen", "Mozambique")

timeseries_df <- meta_data |>
  mutate(
    date = make_date(year, month, 1),
  measles_per_100k = (measles_total / (population * 1000)) * 100000  ) |>
  filter(country %in% c(richest_5, poorest_5)) |>
  mutate(
    wealth_group = case_when(
      country %in% richest_5 ~ "Richest 5",
      country %in% poorest_5 ~ "Poorest 5"
    )
  )
```

```{r}
#| fig-alt: Line plot of measles cases over time comparing five richest countries and five poorest countries from Wikipedia ranking. 

ggplot(timeseries_df, aes(x = date, y = measles_per_100k, group = country, color = wealth_group)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Richest 5" = "steelblue4", "Poorest 5" = "firebrick3")
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Adjusted Measles Rates Over Time (2012-2025)",
    subtitle = "Top 5 richest vs bottom 5 poorest countries",
    x = "Time",
    y = "Measles Cases per 100,000 People",
    color = "Group") +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(face = "bold"),
    legend.position = "bottom")
```

From 2012 to 2025, the poorest five countries experienced higher measles case counts than the richest five countries. While measles cases in the richest group remained close to zero in the past 13 years, the poorest group showed repeated outbreak spikes, with large increases in more recent times. This suggests a strong association GDP and measles cases.

While the time series plot compares measles rates between the richest and poorest country groups, it does not show the size of the economic difference between those groups. The following table will take a deeper dive into this discrepancy. 

```{r}
gdppc_for_countries <- function(data, rich_countries, poor_countries) {
  data |>
    mutate(
      gdp_per_capita = (gdp / population) * 1000,
      group = case_when(
        country %in% rich_countries ~ "Richest 5",
        country %in% poor_countries ~ "Poorest 5")) |>
    filter(country %in% c(rich_countries, poor_countries)) |>
    group_by(group, country) |>
    arrange(desc(year), .by_group = TRUE) |>
    summarize(
      gdp_per_capita_usd = first(gdp_per_capita),
      .groups = "drop") |>
    arrange(group, desc(gdp_per_capita_usd)) |>
    gt(groupname_col = "group") |>
    fmt_currency(
      columns = gdp_per_capita_usd,
      currency = "USD",
      decimals = 0) |>
    cols_label(
      country = "Country",
      gdp_per_capita_usd = "GDP per Capita (USD)") |>
    tab_header(
      title = md("**GDP per Capita of Selected Richest and Poorest Countries**"),
      subtitle = "Countries grouped using GDP per capita rankings") |>
    tab_source_note(
      source_note = "GDP per capita was calculated as GDP divided by population.
      GDP appears to be stored in millions of USD, and population appears to be stored in thousands.") |>
    tab_options(table.width = pct(65))}

gdppc_for_countries(meta_data, richest_5, poorest_5)
```

It is important to note that because the countries were grouped using GDP per capita rankings, an economic gap between the two groups is expected. However, the size of this gap is not immediately visible from the time series plot alone. Based on the table, the selected richest countries have approximately 150 times higher GDP per capita than the selected poorest countries, highlighting the large economic difference between the groups.

## Data Model

```{r}
#| warning: false

linearreg_df <- meta_data |>
  select(
    country,
    year,
    gdp,
    population,
    measles_total,
    mean_temp,
    urban_population,
    tourists) |>
  mutate(
    gdp_per_capita = gdp / population,
    measles_rate_100k = (measles_total / population) * 100000,
    log_gdp_per_capita = log(gdp_per_capita),
    log_measles_rate_100k = log(measles_rate_100k + 1),
    log_tourists = log(tourists))
```

```{r}
  lm(
    log_measles_rate_100k ~ log_gdp_per_capita + mean_temp + log_tourists + urban_population, 
    data = linearreg_df) |>
  tidy() |>
  gt() |>
  fmt_number(
    columns = c(estimate, std.error, statistic, p.value),
    decimals = 3
  ) |>
  cols_label(
    term = md("**Predictor**"),
    estimate = md("**Estimate**"),
    std.error = md("**Std. Error**"),
    statistic = md("**t-value**"),
    p.value = md("**p-value**")
  ) |>
  tab_header(
    title = md("**Regression Model Results**"),
    subtitle = "Predicting logged measles case rate per 100,000"
  ) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = "Linear regression using GDP per capita, mean temperature, number of tourists, and urban population as predictors.")
```

## Original

**To be answered by the data:**

-   How does the volume of measles cases change over time?

-   Which areas of the world contribute the most to measles cases?

**To be answered using supplemental data:**

-   How does vaccination rates affect measles cases by country? (using vaccination data sets likely from WHO)

-   Are measles case rates correlated with GPD and development level of a country? (get GDP and country data from somewhere like gapminder)

**Summary 2**\
- Using measels_year, which regions of the world contribute the most to measles cases? (only looking at 2025)

-   Use of custom function, iteration, 1 unmodified column, 1 modified column

**Summary 3:** Average lab quality across each year.

Average lab quality is the proportion of lab confirmed measles cases and total reported measles cases (across all testing types).

```{r}
years <- sort(unique(cases_year$year))

get_lab_quality <- function(y){
  cases_year |>
    filter(year == y)|>
    mutate(lab_quality = measles_lab_confirmed / measles_total) |>
    summarise(avg_lab_quality = mean(lab_quality, na.rm = TRUE),
              .groups = "drop") |>
    mutate(year = y)
}

map(years, get_lab_quality) |>
  bind_rows() |>
  select(year, avg_lab_quality) |>
  gt() |>
  tab_header(title = md("**Lab Confirmation Quality Over Time**")) |>
  fmt_percent(columns = avg_lab_quality, decimals = 1) |>
  cols_label(
    year = "Year",
    avg_lab_quality = "Average Lab Confirmation Share") |>
  tab_style(style = cell_text(weight = "bold"),
            locations = cells_column_labels()) |>
  tab_source_note(
    source_note = md("*Table 3: Average lab confirmation share by year (2012–2025)*"))
```

Is there a relationship between mean temperature measles cases throughout the year?

```{r}
meta_data |>
  group_by(month, month_abb) |>
  summarize(
    mean_measles = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    mean_temp = mean(mean_temp, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  mutate(
    mean_measles = as.numeric(scale(mean_measles)),
    mean_temp = as.numeric(scale(mean_temp)),
    month_abb = factor(month_abb, levels = month.abb)) |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(mean_measles, mean_temp),
    names_to = "variable",
    values_to = "value") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = month_abb, y = value, color = variable, group = variable)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  labs(
    title = "Measles Cases and Temperature by Month",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Standardized Value",
    color = "Variable") +
  theme_minimal()
```

Is there a relationship between a country's GDP and Measles cases?

```{r}
meta_data |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarize(
    mean_measles = mean(measles_total, na.rm = TRUE),
    gdp_per_capita = mean(gdp_per_capita, na.rm = TRUE),
    .groups = "drop") |>
  filter(mean_measles != 0 & gdp_per_capita != 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_measles)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, size = 3) +
  scale_x_log10() +
  scale_y_log10() +
  labs(
    title = "Country GDP per Capita and Mean Measles Cases",
    x = "GDP per Capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean Measles Cases (log scale)") +
  theme_minimal()
```

```{r}
#| warning: false
library(scales)

meta_data |>
  group_by(country, year) |>
  summarise(annual_measles = sum(measles_total, na.rm=TRUE),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region.x),
            .groups = "drop") |>
  group_by(country) |>
  summarise(mean_annual_measles = mean(annual_measles),
            gdp_per_capita = first(gdp_per_capita),
            region = first(region),
            .grpups = "drop") |>
  ggplot(aes(x = gdp_per_capita, y = mean_annual_measles)) +
  geom_hex(bins = 20) +
  scale_x_log10(labels = dollar_format()) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = comma_format()) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "blueviolet",
                      high = "goldenrod") +
  labs(
    title = "GDP per Capita vs Mean Annual Measles Cases by Country",
    x = "GDP per capita (log scale)",
    y = "Mean annual measles cases (log scale)",
    fill = "No. Countries",
    alt = paste(
      "Hexbin plot of countries comparing GDP per capita and mean annual measles
      cases.",
      "Both axes are on log scale.",
      "Contries are concentrated along a negatively correlated line, with many 
      contries having low GDP and high measles cases, while many others have low
      measles cases and high GDP."
    )
  ) +
  theme_bw() 

```
